speaker: interviewer
text: Today is February 26, 1995. This is an interview with Lillian Eckstein. I'm Amy Friedman, the interviewer. We are in New York, in the United States of America. The interview's in English, and we are on tape five. OK. Do you want to share what you have with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1994, I took a trip to Auschwitz-Birkenau. I took some stones out from the crematorium. Number one, this is the ashes from the crematorium. This is crematorium number two, the ashes. Crematorium number three. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] [SOBBING] This is from the children's crematorium. This is the gas chambers. Auschwitz-Birkenau. This is the kaddish from Jerusalem. I keep it among them. That's all I have to show for my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to go back to Auschwitz for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very difficult. But before I die, I wanted to see how they managed to kill so many people, 1,500 people every 20 minutes. I wanted to see the place where we were. I spend there two days walking around. And I heard the screams, the cries. I couldn't believe it. It was very, very difficult for me-- [SOBBING] to witness this, how something like this can happen in the 20th century, and the world stood by and didn't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any memory that you didn't share with us before, about Auschwitz, that you wanted to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We heard, one night, an explosion-- a very big explosion. And we didn't know what was happening. And the kapo from the camp told us that one of the crematorium was exploded from the partisans from the underground. They came in from the mountains. And they exploded the crematoriums. And that was the beginning, where the Germans realized that there's something-- that they are surrounded and that the world is watching them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you found this out?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's when they started to evacuate people from Auschwitz, into Germany, because they realized-- they knew that something was happening. And we were hoping-- we were hoping that the war was coming to an end, or something is coming to an end, and that the explosion was the beginning of their downfall. That's what I wanted to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can we see your pictures now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is the man to your right?

speaker: interviewee
text: This man is my husband for 40 years. I love him dearly. He's the father of my children. And we are very happy together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael-- Michael-- Michael Eckstein. And I'm very happy to have my wife-- to be my wife, for Lillian to be my wife. I consider it to be very lucky to meet her. And the first sight I saw her, before I even asked her name, I said, you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us that story?

speaker: interviewee
text: A friend of ours told me-- a very good friend of ours, an old friend, from Europe, he told me he has a very good, nice girl. And he gave me the phone number. So I decided to call her up. It was a Tuesday, I remember. And as soon as I called her up, I says, I would like to meet you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so she said, where? I says, how about tonight? So I go up-- we were living in a tenement in the Bronx. I go up. She opens the door. She had an overcoat, some kind of coat on-- a robe. She opens the door. Before I even ask her name, I says to her, you're going to be my wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Very nice proposal, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought he was off the wall. Well, anyway, I inquired about him. And I found out that he comes from a very noble family, he's a very good-natured person, and that he is a good prospect. So after inquiring about him, I decided to take his proposal.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Michael, do you want to tell us--

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I really consider myself to be very lucky. And I wish all of my good friends should wind up like I wound up. She's one of the most wonderful girls there is.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. I love you, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: In every consideration-- she is a very good mother. The kids love her. And as you see this house, she takes care of everything. She's a wonderful person. And I just hope my kids are going to wind up like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we have a visitor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Should we stop? Oh, can we-- come on in. Come-- why don't you come-- [LAUGHTER] Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, take her in your hands-- take her in your hands. [GIRL FUSSING]

speaker: interviewee
text: Come here, sweetie.

speaker: interviewee
text: She'll be more quiet. OK, take her in your hands.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where is Etan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where is Etan?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sledding.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sledding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Take her in your hands. Or you take her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, why don't we cut.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, Michael or Lillian, can you tell us who arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our daughter, Irene, and our favorite granddaughter, Avital. [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] is your name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, Do you want to tell us anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: She is our pride and joy, and we love her dearly.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to tell us about your daughter a little bit? How old is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hold on. Let her sit down.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's three. She just turned three. Yeah. I have another one who is five and a half, and I'm expecting in another month, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: A boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you do? Tell us  about yourself a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell them. I told--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how much she filled-- did she tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mommy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am the youngest, her youngest daughter. And I have another sister, who's in Texas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have a husband and two children. But I do work. I'm a nurse-- an emergency room nurse, and I do that occasionally, when I can, when it's comfortable enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any thoughts on why your mom survived what she did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I have no idea. I have no idea what happened to her during the  war. So I have no idea how she came out of it. We never spoke about it. I never asked. I know that she went through it, but I don't know what happened. I somehow have this idea that I was never supposed to ask. It's something that was taboo. It was never to be brought up. So we never spoke about it. What I do know, I learned from college courses, just being around. I mean, you have to have your head in the sand, especially in New York, not to know what had happened during the Holocaust. But I have no idea what happened to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH] ever wonder where your grandparents were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: But you know where they are, what happened  to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know that they died--

speaker: interviewee
text: You should know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I know they died during the war. Yeah, I know that they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have one last question. Does anybody want to explain that picture behind you guys?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --about 10 years ago. And I was modeling clothes for Alexander's Department Store, fur coats. Somebody saw me in the store, and they suggested that I come in and model clothes for them. And that picture was taken. Turn her around so they can see her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Yeah, let's go back to Avital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Turn her around, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll just say hello, and then we'll be done.

speaker: interviewee
text: She is our love and joy, our daughters and our grandchildren. Without them, really, life would be very lonely. And we are very happy with them and thank God they turned out to be wonderful children-- really wonderful children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who does Avital take after in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you talking about disposition or looks?

speaker: interviewer
text: Whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank God she--

speaker: interviewee
text: You. I think my mother, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank God she looks like her grandmother. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even if I'm good looking, but not for a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great. Thank you. That's wonderful. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister-- my mother's sister. Her name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And she was a very beautiful woman. She had blue eyes, blonde hair, and she won the beauty contest in Carpathia. She died of a horrible death. They burned her in Auschwitz with her son.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest sister, Makala, [NON-ENGLISH]. She was gassed [SOBBING] in Birkenau, with her two children, in 1944. May she rest in peace. She was a young, beautiful woman we lost through a tragic death. She had two small children, and they died in the gas chamber. This is my mother's brother. He died in the United States. He's the one who brought me out to this country. He was a wonderful person. He died of a heart attack in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Rosenberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam Rosenberg. This is my brother, Jack, Yankala. And this is me, just before the ghetto. He died a tragic death. I loved him dearly. May he rest in peace. His name was Jack Yankala Mendelson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year was around 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, 1940. This is me, Lilly Eckstein Mendelson, just before the ghetto in 1942, '43. I was still in school. This is me, in 1942, and [NON-ENGLISH] before the war. Lilly Mendelson Eckstein. This is me, Lilly Eckstein Mendelson, just before coming to the United States. It's in a displaced DP camp near Munich, Germany, just before I came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Ampfing, by Muhldorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm wearing a suit that was given to me by a social worker-- or she was a volunteer from the United Jewish Appeal. So I came to the United States in this outfit. And I was very proud of it, as you see. This is my husband, Michael Eckstein. We've been married 41 years-- very happily married. He's a very good-natured person-- except he snores a little. Otherwise, he's very, very devoted and very good to me. And I love him dearly. And I hope we spend 10, 15 years more together, if his health will permit. Unfortunately, he's not well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken, I believe, in the Bronx in 1975 or '80, some around that time. I don't know what year this was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: It doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: It doesn't matter? This was taken in Washington, DC, at the survivor's meetings. And this is our younger daughter, Irene. And this is me, Lillian Eckstein. And this was a reunion for the Holocaust survivors in Washington. This is our younger daughter, Irene. We love her, and we're very proud of her-- and her degree, a nurse's license. And she works in New York. And we love her dearly. This is Marilyn Eckstein. Dr. Marilyn Eckstein-Levy. She's a doctor. This is her degree-- her doctor's degree-- from Southwestern Medical School. We're very proud of her, and we love her dearly. She's very successful in her field. She specializes in infectious diseases, in Dallas, Texas, in Parkland Hospital. And we wish her all the luck for her life in the future. This is our daughter, Dr. Marilyn Eckstein-Levy. And it's her son, Jonathan Levy. He's a wonderful little boy, as you see. And we love them dearly. This is Marilyn's wedding. She's married to Dr. Moshe Levy. He's a nephrologist. He practices in Dallas, Texas. And this picture was done in New York, in Central Park. And towards the end, this is Michael, my husband, Irene at the far end, and that's me, next to Moshe-- Lillian Eckstein. And to your left, there is my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oops.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to your left, there is my sister, [NON-ENGLISH]. I was with her in Auschwitz all through deliberation. In the middle, there is my sister, Ella, who was taken from Bergen-Belsen to Sweden. I wasn't aware of her whereabouts, and I was lighting candles for years. And then, there is me, Lillian Eckstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is the one on the right the one who married the Polish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's not here. She's dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK. She's not in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are three sisters, survivors. That was our daughter's wedding. The left is me, Lillian. The middle is Ella. And the right-hand side is [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: The middle is my husband, Michael Eckstein. On the left-hand side is his brother-- Michael Eckstein's brother. Rabbi Eckstein lives in Borough Park in Brooklyn. And right-hand side is his brother, Zoli, who lives in Caracas, Venezuela. These are three brothers. This is in Jerusalem, in Yad Vashem. I believe it was in 1984, '85. This is the monument from Bergen-Belsen, where I finally got my freedom. That was Bergen-Belsen, Germany. This is the plaque that I received from United Jewish Appeal, where I was named Woman of the Year. That was done in Manhattan, in Americana Hotel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Here? It says 1986.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1986. This gentleman was named, by the same organization, the Man of the Year, and I was Woman of the Year from Bnai Zion in 1986, in New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was given to me by United Jewish Appeal, by working for the Israeli bonds, in 1983. It was done in New York City, in the Americana hotel. And I was very proud of this, to receive this award.

speaker: interviewer
text: How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for the UJA for the bond-- I sold bonds. And I was very successful at it. They awarded me this, and I'm very proud of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are our grandchildren, our pride and joy. This is, starting with Jessica Levy, Marilyn's daughter. Then we have Etan, Irene's son. Then we have Jonathan, Marilyn's son. And then, we have Avital, Irene's daughter. We live for these big children. We love them dearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about each one, a little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jessica attends private school in Dallas, Texas. She is at the Akiba Hebrew Academy. She is a very good student. Etan goes to New Jersey, in a private school, in a yeshiva. Jonathan is Dallas, in Akiba Hebrew Academy. And Avital is not in school yet. And I hope they follow their parents' footsteps. This is my husband, Michael Eckstein, my daughter, Dr. Marilyn Eckstein-Levy, myself, Lillian Eckstein, and Jessica Levy, Marilyn's daughter. It was, then, in 1990, in Dallas, Texas. And we're very proud of them. This is a picture taken in Dallas, 1992. This is Dr. Moshe Levy. He's a nephrologist in Dallas, Texas. Dr. Marilyn Eckstein-Levy. This is myself, Lillian Eckstein, and Jessica Levy, and Jonathan Levy. This is Marilyn's family, and this is our family, and we are very, very proud of them. This is Marilyn's daughter, Jessica Levy. She is named after my brother, Jack. Her name is [NON-ENGLISH] Jessica Levy. Jessica, you are our oldest granddaughter. You're our first granddaughter. We love you dearly. Please keep close to us. We hope and pray that you will grow up to be a wonderful little girl, just like your mother was. Please keep in touch. Don't forget who we are. We love you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Etan. This is Irene's son, Etan Levy. We love you dearly, Etan-- you know. And this is his sister, Avital. I love you. Say, I love you, Bubbe. Take your hands out of your mouth and say, I love you, Bubbe. Can you give me some [HEBREW SPOKEN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Say, I love you, Bubbe.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you, Bubbe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Give me a big kiss. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you give me a kiss. And one over here. Say, Bubbe, I will always be close to you. Say, I will always be close to you. I will?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Close to me. And as long as you give me chewing gum, and you always have room in my heart and in my home. And I will always love you, Bubbe. I will?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you, Bubbe. OK, take me around and give me a nice kiss. OK. See yourself in there? Say, Bubbe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bubbe.

speaker: interviewee
text: I love you. I love you. I love you. Say, I love you, Bubbe. [LAUGHTER]
